schools.summary: A famine drives Isaac to Gerar: 'and there was a famine in the land, beside the first famine that was in the days of Abraham. And Isaac went unto Abimelech king of the Philistines unto Gerar' — a second famine (distinct from the one in Abraham's day, 12:10) coming on the land (the trial of Isaac, as of his father, the providence that drove the patriarch from his dwelling, the testing of faith in the land of promise); Isaac going to Gerar (the Philistine country, where Abraham had sojourned, ch. 20, to Abimelech, the title of the Philistine kings, perhaps a successor of Abraham's Abimelech, the patriarch seeking refuge from the famine, the journey toward, but not into, Egypt, the setting for God's word, vv.2-5); the famine, like Abraham's trials, testing Isaac's faith (the patriarch tempted, as his father, to go down to Egypt for relief, but stayed by God's command, v.2, the trial that occasioned the renewal of the covenant, the testing of the son of promise in the land) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And there was a famine in the land, beside the first famine that was in the days of Abraham. And Isaac went unto Abimelech king of the Philistines unto Gerar
the famine driving Isaac to Gerar
a second famine (12:10), the trial of Isaac as of his father
the testing of the son of promise, the setting for God's word (vv.2-5)

schools.summary: The LORD forbids Isaac to go to Egypt: 'and the LORD appeared unto him, and said, Go not down into Egypt; dwell in the land which I shall tell thee of' — God appearing to Isaac (as to Abraham) with direction in the famine (the covenant-God guiding the son of promise, the appearing that gave the command and the covenant-renewal, vv.2-5, the providence over Isaac); God forbidding Isaac to go down to Egypt — unlike Abraham (who went to Egypt in the first famine, 12:10), Isaac to stay in the land of promise (the command keeping the son of promise in Canaan, the land given to the seed, the trial of faith — to stay in the famine-struck land, trusting God, rather than seek relief in Egypt); 'dwell in the land which I shall tell thee of' (Isaac to dwell where God directed, Gerar, the land of promise, the obedience of faith, staying in the land at God's word, the patriarch kept in Canaan, the providence ordering his sojourn) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And the LORD appeared unto him, and said, Go not down into Egypt; dwell in the land which I shall tell thee of
the LORD forbidding Isaac to go to Egypt
God keeping the son of promise in Canaan (unlike Abraham, 12:10)
the obedience of faith, staying in the famine-struck land of promise

schools.summary: The renewal of the covenant-promise to Isaac: 'sojourn in this land, and I will be with thee, and will bless thee; for unto thee, and unto thy seed, I will give all these countries, and I will perform the oath which I sware unto Abraham thy father' — God bidding Isaac sojourn in the land (Gerar/Canaan) with the promise of his presence and blessing (the assurance, 'I will be with thee,' the great covenant-promise, 28:15; Isaiah 41:10, the blessing on the obedient son of promise); the promise of the land — 'unto thee, and unto thy seed, I will give all these countries' (the renewal to Isaac of the gift of Canaan, to him and his seed, 13:15; 15:18, the covenant-inheritance confirmed to the son); 'I will perform the oath which I sware unto Abraham thy father' (God keeping to Isaac the oath sworn to Abraham, 22:16-18, the covenant continued in the son, the faithfulness of God to his sworn promise, the blessing passing from father to son) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: Sojourn in this land, and I will be with thee, and will bless thee... and I will perform the oath which I sware unto Abraham thy father
the renewal of the covenant-promise to Isaac
God's presence, the land, and the sworn covenant confirmed to the son (13:15; 22:16-18; 28:15)
the blessing passing from father to son

schools.summary: The promise of the seed and the blessing of the nations renewed to Isaac: 'and I will make thy seed to multiply as the stars of heaven, and will give unto thy seed all these countries; and in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed' — God renewing to Isaac the promise of a numberless seed (as the stars, 15:5; 22:17) and the gift of the land (the covenant-promises, the seed and the land, confirmed to the son, the blessing on Isaac's posterity); the great gospel promise — 'in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed' (12:3; 22:18, the blessing of all nations in Isaac's seed, Paul declaring the seed to be Christ, Galatians 3:16, in whom all nations are blessed, the covenant of grace, the gospel promise, confirmed to Isaac); the seed, the land, and the blessing of the nations — the threefold covenant-promise (made to Abraham, 12:2-3; 22:17-18) renewed entire to Isaac (the covenant carried in the son of promise, the gospel, the seed, Christ, confirmed, the line of the Messiah continued in Isaac) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And I will make thy seed to multiply as the stars of heaven... and in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed
the promise of the seed and the blessing of the nations renewed to Isaac
the numberless seed, the land, and the gospel promise (15:5; 12:3; 22:18)
the seed, Christ (Galatians 3:16), the line of the Messiah continued in Isaac

schools.summary: Abraham's obedience the ground of the blessing: 'because that Abraham obeyed my voice, and kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws' — God giving Abraham's obedience as the reason for renewing the covenant to Isaac (Abraham obeying God's voice, in all things — the call, 12:1; the offering of Isaac, 22:18, 'because thou hast obeyed my voice,' the obedience of faith the occasion of the blessing on the son); the heaping of legal terms — 'charge, commandments, statutes, laws' — describing Abraham's full, comprehensive obedience (the question how Abraham kept 'commandments, statutes, laws' before the written law, Sinai, was given: (a) the terms used generally/anachronistically, describing the patriarch's wholehearted obedience to whatever God commanded him, the obedience of faith; (b) Abraham keeping the law written on the heart, the moral law, Romans 2:14-15, and the precepts and rites God gave him, circumcision, sacrifice; (c) some, the Jewish reading, taking it of the whole law, as if Abraham kept it by anticipation, Calvin noting the obedience commended, yet not as the meritorious cause — the covenant resting on grace, 15:6, Abraham believed God, and his obedience the fruit and evidence of faith, the way in which the blessing is continued, not its purchase); Abraham's obedience set before Isaac, and all, as an example ('do thou do so too,' the blessing flowing in the channel of obedience, the obedient walk of faith the way of the covenant, yet the promise itself of free grace, the obedience the condition/fruit, not the merit) — Gill, Calvin, Clarke, Wesley.

debates.question: How did Abraham keep 'commandments, statutes, laws,' and how does his obedience relate to the covenant?

sides.position: The terms used generally of his full obedience of faith, or of the law written on the heart and the precepts God gave him (Romans 2:14-15) - before the written law of Sinai
The obedience is not the meritorious cause but the fruit and evidence of faith, the channel of the blessing - the covenant resting on grace (15:6)

themes: Because that Abraham obeyed my voice, and kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws
Abraham's obedience as the ground of the blessing (the crux of obedience and covenant)
how Abraham kept commandments, statutes, laws before the written law (the law on the heart, Romans 2:14-15)
the obedience the fruit and channel of the blessing, the covenant resting on grace (15:6)

schools.summary: Isaac dwells in Gerar in obedience: 'and Isaac dwelt in Gerar' — Isaac obeying God's command (v.2, dwell in the land), settling in Gerar (the Philistine country, the obedience of faith, staying in the land of promise rather than going to Egypt, the patriarch dwelling where God directed, the setting for the incident with Abimelech, vv.7-11); Isaac dwelling in Gerar — in obedience to God's word (v.2, the son of promise kept in the land, the trial of faith, to dwell in the famine-struck land, trusting God, the obedience that brought God's blessing, v.12); Isaac's dwelling in Gerar at God's command setting the scene for the chapter — the deception (vv.7-11), the prosperity (vv.12-14), the strife over wells (vv.15-22, the patriarch's sojourn under God's guidance, the obedience of faith carried out) — Gill, Henry.

themes: And Isaac dwelt in Gerar
Isaac dwelling in Gerar in obedience
the obedience of faith, staying in the land at God's command (v.2)
the setting for the events that follow (vv.7-22)

schools.summary: Isaac deceives about his wife: 'and the men of the place asked him of his wife; and he said, She is my sister: for he feared to say, She is my wife; lest, said he, the men of the place should kill me for Rebekah; because she was fair to look upon' — Isaac, asked about Rebekah, saying she was his sister (the same deception his father Abraham had twice practiced, with Sarah, 12:13; 20:2, the strange repetition — Isaac falling into the very fault of his father, the dissembling of the patriarch); Isaac 'feared to say, She is my wife; lest... the men of the place should kill me for Rebekah; because she was fair to look upon' (the same fear of man, Proverbs 29:25, that had driven Abraham, Isaac fearing he would be killed for his fair wife, the weakness of faith, looking to the danger rather than trusting God's protection, who had just promised, v.3, I will be with thee, the deception born of fear, the saying 'she is my sister' a lie, Rebekah not his sister, but his wife and cousin, unlike Sarah, Abraham's half-sister, 20:12, so Isaac's word had no half-truth to lean on, the deception more plainly a falsehood, though some note the loose use of 'sister' for kinswoman); Isaac's repeating his father's sin showing the strange persistence of the same fault in the family (the inherited weakness, the children stumbling where the fathers stumbled, the warning that even the godly fall into the faults of their parents, the deception that endangered Rebekah and the promise, yet overruled by God, vv.8-11) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

debates.question: How is Isaac's 'She is my sister' to be judged?

sides.position: The same fault of unbelief and the fear of man as Abraham's (12:13; 20:2; Proverbs 29:25) - the strange repetition of the father's sin, the weakness of faith despite God's promise (v.3)
A plainer falsehood than Abraham's (Rebekah his wife and cousin, not his sister, with no half-truth as Abraham had, 20:12), though some note the loose use of 'sister' for kinswoman

themes: And the men of the place asked him of his wife; and he said, She is my sister: for he feared to say, She is my wife...
Isaac's deception about his wife (the crux of the repeated fault)
the same fault of unbelief and the fear of man as Abraham's (12:13; 20:2; Proverbs 29:25)
the weakness of faith despite God's just-given promise (v.3)

schools.summary: Abimelech discovers they were married: 'and it came to pass, when he had been there a long time, that Abimelech king of the Philistines looked out at a window, and saw, and, behold, Isaac was sporting with Rebekah his wife' — after Isaac had dwelt long in Gerar (the deception, v.7, maintained a while), Abimelech happening to look out a window and see (the providence that exposed the deception in time, the long sojourn before the discovery); Abimelech seeing Isaac 'sporting with Rebekah his wife' (Isaac showing toward Rebekah such affection, caressing, conjugal endearment, as revealed she was his wife, not his sister, the deception exposed by Isaac's own conduct, the providence uncovering the truth, the husband's familiarity betraying the marriage); Abimelech's seeing the conjugal 'sporting' revealing the truth — that Rebekah was Isaac's wife (the deception, v.7, discovered, the providence of God exposing the fault before harm was done, as with Sarah, ch. 20, the patriarch's dissembling brought to light) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

themes: And it came to pass, when he had been there a long time, that Abimelech... saw, and, behold, Isaac was sporting with Rebekah his wife
Abimelech discovering Isaac and Rebekah were married
the conjugal endearment revealing the marriage, the deception exposed
the providence of God uncovering the fault before harm was done (ch. 20)

schools.summary: Abimelech rebukes Isaac: 'and Abimelech called Isaac, and said, Behold, of a surety she is thy wife: and how saidst thou, She is my sister? And Isaac said unto him, Because I said, Lest I die for her' — Abimelech, having discovered the truth (v.8), reproving Isaac: it was certain Rebekah was his wife, why had he said she was his sister? (the king's just reproof of the deception, the patriarch reproved by the heathen king, as Abraham, 12:18; 20:9); the king's reproof — why the deception? (the just complaint against the falsehood, Isaac humbled, reproved for his fault by Abimelech); Isaac's answer, 'Because I said, Lest I die for her' (the patriarch confessing his fear, that he would be killed for Rebekah, v.7, the fear of man, Proverbs 29:25, that drove the deception, the weak excuse of the godly man, owning the fault born of fear, the same plea as Abraham's, 20:11) — Gill.

themes: And Abimelech called Isaac, and said, Behold, of a surety she is thy wife: and how saidst thou, She is my sister?...
Abimelech rebuking Isaac for the deception
the patriarch reproved by the heathen king (12:18; 20:9)
Isaac confessing the fear of man (Proverbs 29:25) that drove the fault

schools.summary: Abimelech complains of the danger: 'and Abimelech said, What is this thou hast done unto us? one of the people might lightly have lien with thy wife, and thou shouldest have brought guiltiness upon us' — the king reproaching Isaac for the deception that had endangered him and his people ('What is this thou hast done unto us?', the just complaint, as Abraham was reproved, 12:18; 20:9, the patriarch's fault exposed); 'one of the people might lightly (easily) have lien with thy wife' (the deception having endangered Rebekah's honor, one of the people, supposing her unmarried, might have taken her, and brought guilt on the nation, the peril the deception had risked, the danger to the wife and the promise); 'thou shouldest have brought guiltiness upon us' (the king noting that the deception might have brought sin and guilt on his people, had any taken Rebekah unwittingly, the heathen king's sense of the gravity of adultery, the danger the patriarch's fault had risked for the nation, the providence that prevented it, v.11) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And Abimelech said, What is this thou hast done unto us? one of the people might lightly have lien with thy wife, and thou shouldest have brought guiltiness upon us
Abimelech's complaint of the danger the deception had brought
the deception endangering Rebekah and bringing guilt on the nation
the heathen king's sense of the gravity of the fault (12:18; 20:9)

schools.summary: Abimelech's decree protecting Isaac and Rebekah: 'and Abimelech charged all his people, saying, He that toucheth this man or his wife shall surely be put to death' — the king issuing a decree protecting Isaac and Rebekah, on pain of death (the royal protection of the patriarch and his wife, the providence, and the king's justice, securing them, the deception's danger removed by the decree); the charge — none was to harm Isaac or Rebekah (the king's care to protect them, and to prevent the guilt, v.10, the providence guarding the son of promise and his wife, the protection the more remarkable from a heathen king); 'shall surely be put to death' (the severe penalty securing the protection, the king's earnest care, the patriarch and his wife safe under the decree, the providence overruling the deception for their safety, the covenant-couple preserved) — Gill, JFB, Calvin.

themes: And Abimelech charged all his people, saying, He that toucheth this man or his wife shall surely be put to death
Abimelech's decree protecting Isaac and Rebekah
the royal protection securing the patriarch and his wife
the providence overruling the deception for their safety

schools.summary: Isaac's hundredfold harvest, the LORD's blessing: 'then Isaac sowed in that land, and received in the same year an hundredfold: and the LORD blessed him' — Isaac, dwelling in Gerar, sowing and reaping a hundredfold (a vast, extraordinary increase) in the same year (the abundant harvest, the more remarkable in a time of famine, v.1, the prosperity of the patriarch, the blessing on his husbandry); the hundredfold — the extraordinary, God-given increase (the harvest far beyond nature, in a famine-year, the visible token of God's blessing, the prosperity that made Isaac great, vv.13-14); 'the LORD blessed him' (the increase the LORD's blessing, not chance or skill, the fulfillment of God's promise, v.3, I will be with thee, and will bless thee, the blessing on the obedient patriarch, God's faithfulness to his word, the prosperity a gift of God, Deuteronomy 8:18) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: Then Isaac sowed in that land, and received in the same year an hundredfold: and the LORD blessed him
Isaac's hundredfold harvest and the LORD's blessing
the extraordinary increase in a famine-year, the token of God's blessing
the prosperity fulfilling the promise (v.3), a gift of God (Deuteronomy 8:18)

schools.summary: Isaac grows very great: 'and the man waxed great, and went forward, and grew until he became very great' — Isaac growing increasingly great (in substance and prosperity, the threefold note of his growth, waxed great, went forward, became very great, marking the steady, abundant increase, the blessing of God making him great, v.12, the prosperity of the patriarch); the increasing greatness — Isaac going forward (advancing, growing) until very great (the steady increase of his substance, the visible blessing of God, the prosperity that drew the Philistines' envy, v.14); 'until he became very great' (Isaac's prosperity reaching its height, great possessions, v.14, the blessing of God abundantly fulfilled, v.3, the patriarch a great and wealthy man, the greatness that occasioned both envy, v.14, and the world's witness, v.28) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And the man waxed great, and went forward, and grew until he became very great
Isaac growing very great
the steady, God-given increase (v.12) making him a great and wealthy man
the greatness that drew the Philistines' envy (v.14)

schools.summary: Isaac's great possessions, the Philistines' envy: 'for he had possession of flocks, and possession of herds, and great store of servants: and the Philistines envied him' — Isaac's great wealth (flocks, herds, many servants, the abundant substance of the patriarch, the visible token of God's blessing, v.12, the prosperity that made him great, v.13); the 'great store of servants' (a large household, the wealth and power of the patriarch, the great establishment, the prosperity that the Philistines could not but notice); 'the Philistines envied him' (the Philistines, seeing Isaac's prosperity, envying him, the envy of the world at the prosperity of the godly, Psalm 37:1; Proverbs 27:4, the envy that led to the stopping of the wells, v.15, and the request to depart, v.16, the hostility born of envy) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: For he had possession of flocks, and possession of herds, and great store of servants: and the Philistines envied him
Isaac's great possessions and the Philistines' envy
the abundant substance, the visible token of God's blessing (v.12)
the envy of the world at the prosperity of the godly (Psalm 37:1; Proverbs 27:4)

schools.summary: The Philistines stop Abraham's wells out of envy: 'for all the wells which his father's servants had digged in the days of Abraham his father, the Philistines had stopped them, and filled them with earth' — the wells Abraham had dug (precious in the dry land) stopped (filled with earth) by the Philistines (the spiteful act of envy, the destruction of the water-sources, the hostility against Isaac); the Philistines stopping the wells out of envy (v.14) — a spiteful, hostile act (to destroy the wells was a grave injury in the East, the malice that begrudged Isaac the water, the envy turned to deed); 'filled them with earth' (the wells deliberately ruined, the precious water-sources destroyed, the injury that Isaac would patiently repair, v.18, the hostility of the envious Philistines, the trial of the patriarch's peaceable spirit) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: For all the wells which his father's servants had digged in the days of Abraham his father, the Philistines had stopped them, and filled them with earth
the Philistines stopping Abraham's wells out of envy
the precious water-sources deliberately destroyed, a grave injury
the trial of the patriarch's peaceable spirit (which Isaac would patiently repair, v.18)

schools.summary: Abimelech asks Isaac to depart: 'and Abimelech said unto Isaac, Go from us; for thou art much mightier than we' — the king (perhaps pressed by his envious people, v.14) asking Isaac to depart, because he had grown mightier (more numerous and wealthy) than the Philistines (the request born of envy and fear, the patriarch asked to leave, a kind of expulsion, the prosperity that made the Philistines uneasy); the reason — Isaac had become 'much mightier' (greater in substance and household) than the Philistines (the fear and envy of the king and people, the uneasiness at the patriarch's greatness, the request to remove him); 'Go from us' (the king bidding Isaac depart, the patriarch, though wronged, vv.15, peaceably complying, v.17, the trial of the godly, asked to leave for his very prosperity, the providence ordering his removal, to Beersheba, v.23) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Abimelech said unto Isaac, Go from us; for thou art much mightier than we
Abimelech asking Isaac to depart
the request born of envy and fear at the patriarch's greatness
the trial of the godly, peaceably complied with (v.17)

schools.summary: Isaac peaceably departs: 'and Isaac departed thence, and pitched his tent in the valley of Gerar, and dwelt there' — Isaac, asked to leave (v.16), peaceably departing and pitching his tent in the valley (wadi) of Gerar (the patriarch's quiet, peaceable compliance, not resisting, though wronged, the removal to the valley, the dwelling there); Isaac departing peaceably — yielding to the request, avoiding strife (the meek, peaceable spirit of the patriarch, Romans 12:18, the godly man not contending, but removing); 'pitched his tent in the valley of Gerar, and dwelt there' (Isaac settling in the valley, a little removed from the city, the new dwelling, the setting for the digging of the wells, vv.18-22, the patriarch's peaceable sojourn continued) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And Isaac departed thence, and pitched his tent in the valley of Gerar, and dwelt there
Isaac peaceably departing
the patriarch's quiet, peaceable compliance, not contending though wronged (Romans 12:18)
the setting for the digging of the wells (vv.18-22)

schools.summary: Isaac reopens his father's wells: 'and Isaac digged again the wells of water, which they had digged in the days of Abraham his father; for the Philistines had stopped them after the death of Abraham: and he called their names after the names by which his father had called them' — Isaac reopening the wells his father had dug (and the Philistines had stopped, v.15, the patient labor of repairing the injury, the recovering of the water-sources, the patriarch restoring his father's wells); Isaac 'called their names after the names by which his father had called them' (restoring the old names, a token of honoring his father and reclaiming the wells as his inheritance, the continuity with Abraham, the patriarch holding to his father's memory and possession); Isaac's reopening the wells (rather than contending) showing his peaceable, patient spirit (the recovering of the water without strife, the honoring of his father, the patriarch's quiet industry under the trial, the wells restored, yet new strife would come, vv.19-21) — Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Isaac digged again the wells of water, which they had digged in the days of Abraham his father... and he called their names after the names by which his father had called them
Isaac reopening his father's wells
the patient recovering of the water-sources, honoring his father and the continuity with Abraham
the patriarch's quiet, peaceable industry under the trial

schools.summary: The finding of a well of springing water: 'and Isaac's servants digged in the valley, and found there a well of springing water' — Isaac's servants digging in the valley and finding a well of 'springing' (living, running) water (the precious find, living water, the best, a fountain-spring rather than a mere cistern, the gift of water in the dry land, the occasion of new strife, v.20); the 'springing' (living, Hebrew chayyim, living) water — a flowing spring (the most valuable kind of well, the abundant, fresh water, the providence giving Isaac the water, the find that the herdmen of Gerar would dispute, v.20); the finding of living water (the gift of God to the patriarch) setting the scene for the strife (vv.20-21) and Isaac's peaceable yielding (the precious water-source, the providence over Isaac's sojourn, the well that occasioned the naming of Esek, v.20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And Isaac's servants digged in the valley, and found there a well of springing water
the finding of a well of springing water
the precious flowing spring (living water), the gift of God in the dry land
the find that occasioned new strife (v.20)

schools.summary: The strife over the first well, named Esek: 'and the herdmen of Gerar did strive with Isaac's herdmen, saying, The water is ours: and he called the name of the well Esek; because they strove with him' — the herdsmen of Gerar contending with Isaac's servants over the well, claiming the water, Isaac naming the well Esek ('contention,' 'strife') because they strove with him (the first dispute over the wells, the patriarch's trial of patience); the unjust claim of the Gerar herdsmen — disputing Isaac's right to the well his servants had dug (the contention, the envy and hostility, v.14, continuing); 'he called the name of the well Esek; because they strove with him' (Esek, contention, a memorial of the strife, Isaac, rather than contend, naming the well and, as the next verses show, yielding and moving on, the peaceable spirit of the patriarch, not fighting for his right, the first of the wells of strife, Esek, Sitnah, before Rehoboth, v.22) — Gill, Wesley.

themes: And the herdmen of Gerar did strive with Isaac's herdmen, saying, The water is ours: and he called the name of the well Esek; because they strove with him
the strife over the first well, named Esek (contention)
the unjust claim disputing Isaac's right to the well
the patriarch's trial of patience, yielding rather than fighting

schools.summary: The strife over the second well, named Sitnah: 'and they digged another well, and strove for that also: and he called the name of it Sitnah' — Isaac's servants digging a second well, and the herdsmen of Gerar striving for that too, Isaac naming it Sitnah ('hatred,' 'enmity,' 'opposition') because of the contention (the second dispute, the continuing hostility against the patriarch); the strife continuing — the herdsmen disputing the second well as the first (v.20, the persistent hostility, the trial of Isaac's patience); 'he called the name of it Sitnah' (Sitnah, hatred, enmity, the root of 'Satan,' the adversary, a memorial of the opposition, Isaac, again yielding rather than fighting, naming the well and moving on, v.22, the peaceable spirit under repeated provocation, the second of the wells of strife) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And they digged another well, and strove for that also: and he called the name of it Sitnah
the strife over the second well, named Sitnah (hatred)
the persistent opposition, the continuing hostility
the patriarch again yielding rather than fighting

schools.summary: The well of peace, Rehoboth, and Isaac's peaceable spirit: 'and he removed from thence, and digged another well; and for that they strove not: and he called the name of it Rehoboth; and he said, For now the LORD hath made room for us, and we shall be fruitful in the land' — Isaac moving further off and digging a third well, for which there was no strife (the peaceable patriarch, removing rather than contending, at last finding rest, the well undisputed, the end of the strife); Isaac naming the well Rehoboth ('enlargements,' 'room enough') and saying, 'For now the LORD hath made room for us' (Isaac ascribing the peace and room to the LORD, not his own striving, the peaceable spirit of the patriarch — yielding twice, Esek, Sitnah, vv.20-21, not fighting for his right, trusting God to make room, the reward of patience and meekness, Matthew 5:5, the meek inherit the earth, the providence giving room and rest, the example of the godly who, rather than contend, yield and trust God to provide); 'we shall be fruitful in the land' (Isaac's confidence that now, with room, God would make him fruitful and prosper him, the faith and hope of the patriarch, the peace and increase given of God, the fruit of the peaceable spirit) — Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

debates.question: What does the well Rehoboth and Isaac's yielding display?

sides.position: The reward of the peaceable, meek spirit - Isaac yielding twice (Esek, Sitnah) and trusting God to make room, rather than contending for his right; the meek inheriting (Matthew 5:5; Romans 12:18), the LORD giving room and fruitfulness

themes: And he removed from thence, and digged another well; and for that they strove not: and he called the name of it Rehoboth... For now the LORD hath made room for us
the well of peace, Rehoboth, and Isaac's peaceable spirit (the crux of the wells)
Isaac yielding twice (Esek, Sitnah) and trusting God to make room, rather than contending
the reward of meekness - the LORD making room (Matthew 5:5; Romans 12:18)

schools.summary: Isaac goes up to Beersheba: 'and he went up from thence to Beersheba' — Isaac going up (Beersheba being higher, or further into the land) to Beersheba (the place of Abraham's covenant with Abimelech, 21:31-33, and his calling on the everlasting God, the patriarch returning to the place associated with his father and with God's covenant, the setting for the LORD's appearing, v.24); Isaac going from the valley of Gerar (and Rehoboth) up to Beersheba (the removal further into the land, the journey to the place of his father's worship); 'to Beersheba' (the well of the oath, 21:31, where Abraham had dwelt, worshiped, and made covenant, the place of covenant and worship, the providence bringing Isaac there for God's renewed appearing, v.24, and the covenant with Abimelech, vv.26-33, the patriarch in his father's place) — Gill, JFB, Calvin.

themes: And he went up from thence to Beersheba
Isaac going up to Beersheba
the place of Abraham's covenant and worship (21:31-33)
the setting for the LORD's renewed appearing (v.24)

schools.summary: The LORD appears as the God of Abraham: 'and the LORD appeared unto him the same night, and said, I am the God of Abraham thy father: fear not, for I am with thee, and will bless thee, and multiply thy seed for my servant Abraham's sake' — God appearing to Isaac at Beersheba (the night of his coming) and revealing himself as 'the God of Abraham thy father' (the covenant-God of the fathers, Exodus 3:6, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, the renewal of the covenant to the son, the assurance that the God of his father was his God); God saying 'fear not, for I am with thee' (the gracious word of comfort to Isaac, who needed encouragement after the strife and removals, vv.16-22, and the trials, 'fear not' the great word of God to his people, Isaiah 41:10; Genesis 15:1, 'I am with thee' the covenant-presence, the promise renewed, v.3, the assurance that God was with Isaac to bless and protect, the comfort of the covenant-God); 'and will bless thee, and multiply thy seed for my servant Abraham's sake' (God renewing the promise of blessing and a numberless seed, 'for my servant Abraham's sake,' the covenant made with Abraham, now continued to Isaac, the blessing flowing from the covenant with the father, the faithfulness of God to his oath, 22:16-18, the covenant-mercy resting on Isaac for Abraham's sake) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

debates.question: What does the LORD's appearing as 'the God of Abraham' to Isaac display?

sides.position: The covenant-God of the fathers (Exodus 3:6) renewing the covenant to the son - 'fear not, for I am with thee' the word of comfort and the covenant-presence (Isaiah 41:10), the blessing resting on the covenant with Abraham ('for my servant Abraham's sake')

themes: And the LORD appeared unto him the same night, and said, I am the God of Abraham thy father: fear not, for I am with thee...
the LORD appearing to Isaac as the God of Abraham (the crux of the covenant-God)
the covenant-God of the fathers (Exodus 3:6) renewing the covenant to the son
'fear not, for I am with thee' (Isaiah 41:10), the blessing for Abraham's sake

schools.summary: Isaac's worship and settling at Beersheba: 'and he builded an altar there, and called upon the name of the LORD, and pitched his tent there: and there Isaac's servants digged a well' — Isaac, after God's appearing (v.24), building an altar at Beersheba and worshiping (calling on the name of the LORD, the response of faith and gratitude, the patriarch's worship at the place of his father, Abraham had called on the everlasting God there, 21:33, the public profession and worship of God); Isaac 'called upon the name of the LORD' (public worship and prayer, the open profession of the true God, as Abraham, 12:8; 13:4; 21:33, the patriarch's piety, the altar and worship marking his dwelling); 'pitched his tent there: and there Isaac's servants digged a well' (Isaac settling at Beersheba, pitched his tent, and his servants digging a well, the peaceable dwelling, the providence giving water, the patriarch settled in his father's place, worshiping and dwelling, the well that would be named, vv.32-33) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And he builded an altar there, and called upon the name of the LORD, and pitched his tent there: and there Isaac's servants digged a well
Isaac's worship and settling at Beersheba
the response of faith and gratitude after God's appearing (v.24), in his father's place (21:33)
the public profession and worship of God (12:8), the peaceable dwelling and the well

schools.summary: Abimelech comes to seek a covenant: 'then Abimelech went to him from Gerar, and Ahuzzath one of his friends, and Phichol the chief captain of his army' — Abimelech (the Philistine king) coming to Isaac at Beersheba, with Ahuzzath (his friend/counselor) and Phichol (his army-commander, the same name as in Abraham's day, 21:22, perhaps a title, the king seeking a covenant with the prospering patriarch, vv.28-29, the approach of the Philistine ruler); the king's company — his friend (counselor) and his army-captain (the formal embassy, the dignity of the approach, the king seeking peace with Isaac, whom he had sent away, v.16); Abimelech's coming to seek a covenant showing the changed estimate — the king, having sent Isaac away (v.16), now seeking his friendship, perceiving God's blessing on him (v.28, the providence giving the patriarch favor, the parallel with Abraham's covenant with Abimelech, 21:22-32) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: Then Abimelech went to him from Gerar, and Ahuzzath one of his friends, and Phichol the chief captain of his army
Abimelech coming to Isaac to seek a covenant
the king, having sent Isaac away (v.16), now seeking his friendship
perceiving God's blessing on the patriarch (v.28; cf. 21:22)

schools.summary: Isaac's frank question to Abimelech: 'and Isaac said unto them, Wherefore come ye to me, seeing ye hate me, and have sent me away from you?' — Isaac frankly asking why they came, since they had shown hatred (envy, v.14) and sent him away (v.16, the just, plain reproach, the patriarch noting the inconsistency, they had driven him out, now sought him, the honest dealing of Isaac); Isaac charging them with hatred — the envy (v.14) and the expulsion (v.16, the plain statement of the wrong done, the patriarch not dissembling, but speaking honestly of their former hostility); 'have sent me away from you' (Isaac recalling that they had driven him out, v.16, the just reproach, the question that drew out the king's confession of God's blessing on Isaac, v.28, the honest, frank dealing of the patriarch) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And Isaac said unto them, Wherefore come ye to me, seeing ye hate me, and have sent me away from you?
Isaac's frank question to Abimelech
the just reproach, noting their former hatred (v.14) and expulsion (v.16)
the honest question that drew out the king's confession (v.28)

schools.summary: The Philistines confess the LORD was with Isaac: 'and they said, We saw certainly that the LORD was with thee: and we said, Let there be now an oath betwixt us, even betwixt us and thee, and let us make a covenant with thee' — Abimelech and his men confessing that they had plainly seen the LORD was with Isaac (in his prosperity and blessing, vv.12-14, the witness of the world to God's blessing on the godly, the heathen owning the LORD's presence with the patriarch, as with Abraham, 21:22, God is with thee in all that thou doest, the testimony of the Philistines); they sought 'an oath... and a covenant' (a treaty of peace and good faith between them and Isaac, the king, perceiving God's blessing, sought the patriarch's friendship and security, the covenant of peace, as Abraham's with Abimelech, 21:27-32); the Philistines' confession ('the LORD was with thee') the world's witness to the blessing of God on his people (the prosperity of the godly compelling even their enemies to own God's hand, the providence giving Isaac such favor and testimony, the covenant sought on the ground of God's evident blessing) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And they said, We saw certainly that the LORD was with thee... let us make a covenant with thee
the Philistines' confession that the LORD was with Isaac
the world's witness to God's blessing on the godly (21:22)
the covenant of peace sought on the ground of God's evident blessing (21:27-32)

schools.summary: Abimelech seeks peace, owning Isaac blessed of the LORD: 'that thou wilt do us no hurt, as we have not touched thee, and as we have done unto thee nothing but good, and have sent thee away in peace: thou art now the blessed of the LORD' — Abimelech seeking Isaac's pledge of good faith (to do them no hurt), claiming they had not harmed him but done him good and sent him away in peace (the king's, somewhat self-serving, account of their dealing, the seeking of mutual peace and security); the king's claim — that they had sent Isaac away peaceably (v.16, the request for the like good faith in return, the basis of the covenant in mutual peace, though Isaac might have noted the envy and wells, vv.14-21, the patriarch's peaceable spirit accepted the peace); 'thou art now the blessed of the LORD' (Abimelech owning Isaac as blessed of the LORD, as Laban had owned Abraham's servant, 24:31, the heathen king's confession of God's blessing on the patriarch, the ground of the covenant — Isaac's evident blessing, the world's testimony to the godly) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: That thou wilt do us no hurt... and have sent thee away in peace: thou art now the blessed of the LORD
Abimelech seeking a pledge of peace, owning Isaac blessed of the LORD
the king seeking mutual good faith and security
the heathen king's confession of God's blessing on the patriarch (24:31)

schools.summary: Isaac makes a feast for Abimelech: 'and he made them a feast, and they did eat and drink' — Isaac making a feast for Abimelech and his company (the token of friendship and hospitality, the ratifying of the covenant with a feast, the customary sealing of a treaty by eating together, 31:54, the patriarch's gracious reception of the king); Isaac feasting them — a mark of good will and the conclusion of peace (the hospitality of the patriarch, the friendly entertainment of the king); 'they did eat and drink' (the eating together a token of the covenant and the peace, the covenant-meal, as Jacob and Laban, 31:54, the sealing of the treaty by the feast, the friendly fellowship, the way to the oath, v.31, the peace concluded) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And he made them a feast, and they did eat and drink
Isaac making a feast for Abimelech
the token of friendship and the covenant-meal sealing the treaty (31:54)
the patriarch's gracious hospitality concluding the peace

schools.summary: The oath and the peaceable parting: 'and they rose up betimes in the morning, and sware one to another: and Isaac sent them away, and they departed from him in peace' — early in the morning, Isaac and Abimelech swearing mutually (the oath ratifying the covenant of peace, vv.28-29, the treaty sealed by oath, the mutual pledge of good faith, the covenant concluded); the mutual oath — the binding of both parties to peace and good faith (the lawful oath sealing the treaty, as Abraham, 21:31, the covenant of peace between Isaac and the Philistines); 'Isaac sent them away, and they departed from him in peace' (the parties parting in peace, the patriarch dismissing the king with good will, the peaceable conclusion, the contrast with the former hostility, vv.14-16, Isaac at peace with his neighbors, Romans 12:18, the covenant of peace established) — Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And they rose up betimes in the morning, and sware one to another: and Isaac sent them away, and they departed from him in peace
the oath and the peaceable parting
the mutual oath sealing the covenant of peace (vv.28-29; 21:31)
Isaac at peace with his neighbors (Romans 12:18)

schools.summary: The finding of water the day of the covenant: 'and it came to pass the same day, that Isaac's servants came, and told him concerning the well which they had digged, and said unto him, We have found water' — on the very day of the covenant (vv.30-31), Isaac's servants reporting they had found water in the well they dug (v.25, the timely gift of water, the providence blessing the patriarch, water in the dry land, on the day of peace, the well that would name the place, v.33); the well Isaac's servants had been digging (v.25) — now yielding water (the providence rewarding the patriarch's industry, the gift of water at Beersheba); 'We have found water' (the finding of water, the precious gift in the dry land, on the day of the covenant, the providence crowning the day of peace with the gift, the well that, with the oath, v.31, gave Beersheba its name, v.33, the blessing of God on Isaac) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And it came to pass the same day, that Isaac's servants came, and told him concerning the well which they had digged, and said unto him, We have found water
the finding of water the day of the covenant
the timely gift of water (in the dry land) on the day of peace
the providence crowning the day, the well that would name Beersheba (v.33)

schools.summary: The naming of the well Shebah, whence Beersheba: 'and he called it Shebah: therefore the name of the city is Beersheba unto this day' — Isaac naming the well Shebah ('oath,' or 'seven,' echoing both the oath, v.31, and Abraham's earlier naming, 21:31, the well named for the covenant-oath, and the water found, the place called Beersheba, 'well of the oath,' or 'well of seven,' to this day, the renewal of the name his father had given, 21:31); Shebah ('oath,' or 'seven') echoing Abraham's Beersheba (21:31) — the well of the oath (Isaac renewing the name, or confirming it, on occasion of his own covenant-oath with Abimelech, the memorial of the oath and the peace); 'the name of the city is Beersheba unto this day' (Beersheba, the well of the oath/seven, a notable place in the patriarchal history, 21:31-33; 28:10, the southern limit of the land, 'from Dan to Beersheba,' the name confirmed by Isaac's covenant, the place of his father's and his own oath and worship) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And he called it Shebah: therefore the name of the city is Beersheba unto this day
the naming of the well Shebah, whence Beersheba
the well of the oath (and seven), renewing his father's name (21:31)
the memorial of Isaac's covenant with Abimelech and of the peace

schools.summary: Esau marries Hittite wives: 'and Esau was forty years old when he took to wife Judith the daughter of Beeri the Hittite, and Bashemath the daughter of Elon the Hittite' — Esau, at forty (the age at which his father Isaac had married, 25:20), taking two Hittite wives (Judith and Bashemath, daughters of the Canaanites, the profane Esau, 25:34; Hebrews 12:16, marrying outside the covenant, into the accursed line of Canaan, the disregard of the family's care, which had sought a wife for Isaac among the kindred, not the Canaanites, 24:3); the wives Hittites (Canaanites, of the accursed line, 9:25, Esau, contrary to the example and care of Abraham and Isaac, who shunned Canaanite marriages, 24:3, marrying into the Canaanites, the profane disregard of the covenant, the marriages that grieved his parents, v.35); Esau's marrying Hittite (Canaanite) wives of a piece with his despising the birthright (25:34) — the profane man, careless of the covenant, marrying into the line under the curse (the contrast with Jacob, who would seek a wife of the kindred, 28:1-2, the further evidence of Esau's unfitness for the covenant, the marriages a grief to Isaac and Rebekah, v.35) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Esau was forty years old when he took to wife Judith the daughter of Beeri the Hittite, and Bashemath the daughter of Elon the Hittite
Esau's marriage to Hittite wives
the profane man marrying into the accursed line of Canaan, contrary to his fathers' care (24:3; 9:25)
of a piece with his despising the birthright (25:34; Hebrews 12:16)

schools.summary: The grief of Isaac and Rebekah over Esau's wives: 'which were a grief of mind unto Isaac and to Rebekah' — Esau's Hittite wives (v.34) a grief (bitterness of spirit) to his parents (the sorrow of Isaac and Rebekah at the Canaanite marriages, contrary to the covenant-care, 24:3, the bitter trial of godly parents over an ungodly son's choices, the grief at the profane match); the 'grief of mind' (bitterness of spirit) — the deep sorrow of the parents (the Canaanite wives a continual vexation, and, later, occasion of Rebekah's plan to send Jacob away, 27:46, the grief of the godly over the unequal, profane marriages); the grief of Isaac and Rebekah a warning — the sorrow that ungodly marriages (into the world, against the covenant) bring to godly parents (the bitter fruit of Esau's profanity, 25:34; v.34, the contrast with the care for Jacob's marriage, 28:1-2, the grief that would shape the next chapter, 27:46) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, JFB, Clarke.

themes: Which were a grief of mind unto Isaac and to Rebekah
the grief of Isaac and Rebekah over Esau's wives
the sorrow of godly parents over the Canaanite, covenant-disregarding marriages (24:3)
the bitter fruit of Esau's profanity (25:34), shaping the next chapter (27:46)
